result:
# The Unremarkable Tuesday

The coffee was bad. Not offensive, not remarkable, just bad in the way that gas station coffee is bad—thin and bitter, with a burnt aftertaste that clings to the roof of your mouth like an unwanted opinion. I drank it anyway.

The woman at the counter had given me a look when I ordered. Not mean, exactly. More like she was trying to place me. Former classmate? Friend of a friend? Someone she'd seen on the news for something unfortunate? I could see her working through the possibilities behind her eyes, and I wanted to tell her: don't strain yourself. I'm nobody you know. I'm barely somebody I know.

Outside, the rain had stopped but left its evidence everywhere. Puddles on the sidewalk reflected a sky that couldn't decide what it wanted to be. The air smelled like wet asphalt and someone's dryer exhaust, and I thought: this is the smell of Tuesday. Of all the Tuesdays. Of every ordinary morning in every ordinary place where people are just trying to get to the next ordinary thing.

I sat in my car for nine minutes before going inside to work. Nine minutes of watching people cross the parking lot. A man in a suit carrying a dry-cleaning bag over his shoulder like a rifle. A woman with a stroller, one wheel stuck, yanking it sideways with the kind of controlled frustration that comes from practice. Two teenagers sharing earbuds, laughing at something on a phone screen, their whole world contained in a six-inch rectangle.

None of them knew. How could they?

---

My mother called at lunch. She always calls at lunch because she knows I'll answer. Not out of duty—I just don't have the heart to let it ring while she's sitting in that kitchen, surrounded by all those photographs of a family that doesn't really exist anymore. Not the way the photos suggest. Not gathered and smiling and intact.

"Did you eat?" she asked.

"I'm eating."

"What are you eating?"

"A sandwich."

"What kind?"

"Turkey."

"From the deli or the package?"

I closed my eyes. "From the deli, Mom."

"Good. The packaged kind has too much sodium. Your father—"

She stopped. She always stops there now. It's been four years, and she still catches herself mid-sentence, like stepping off a curb before realizing the street is gone. I used to think the catching was the hard part. Now I think it's the not catching. The times she gets all the way through the sentence and doesn't notice she's speaking to someone who should be listening from the other room.

"I know, Mom," I said. "The packaged kind has too much sodium."

---

After work, I drove past the house. I don't know why I keep doing this. Habit, maybe. Or the same impulse that makes you press on a bruise—not to reopen the wound but to confirm it's still there. That the pain wasn't imagined.

The new owners painted the door red. My father would have hated it. He spent three weekends once refinishing that door, stripping it down to bare wood and building the stain back up in thin layers until it looked like honey held to light. "A door should look like it means it," he said. I didn't know what that meant then. I still don't, not exactly. But I think about it all the time.

The yard looks smaller now. They took out the maple tree—the one with the tire swing, the one my sister fell out of when she was nine and somehow didn't break anything, just lay in the grass laughing with shock while I screamed. I remember my father coming out, calm as Sunday, checking her limbs one by one like he was inspecting produce. "You're fine," he said. "Stop scaring your brother."

I wasn't scared for her. I was scared because I'd seen her fall and for one second—just one—she hadn't looked like a person. She'd looked like something dropping through air without a plan. Like all of us, I guess. Dropping through air without a plan and hoping someone will check our limbs afterward.

---

At home, I heated soup. I stood in the kitchen and ate it straight from the pot because no one was watching and because the bowl felt like a commitment I wasn't ready to make. The soup was fine. Tomato. Too salty, probably—the packaged kind.

The apartment was quiet in that specific way apartments are quiet when you live alone. Not silent. The fridge hummed. A pipe knocked somewhere inside the wall. A neighbor's television murmured through the ceiling like someone describing a dream you can't quite hear.

I thought about calling someone. Not anyone specific—just the act of calling. Of reaching into the silence and finding another voice. But who? My sister lives in Portland now. We text sometimes, brief dispatches from our separate lives. "How's work?" "Fine." "Did you see Mom?" "Yeah, she's good." Two people on opposite sides of a canyon, tossing pebbles and listening for the landing.

My phone buzzed. A news alert. Something happening somewhere to people I'd never meet. I swiped it away and stared at the blank screen and saw my own face, ghostly and indistinct, looking back.

---

In bed, I read twelve pages of a novel before the words stopped arranging themselves into meaning. The book was about a man returning to his hometown. I'd picked it for that reason—research, I told myself, like returning to a place could ever be studied instead of survived.

I turned off the light. The room went dark but not black—there's never true darkness in a city, just varying shades of orange and gray filtering in from everywhere and nowhere.

Tomorrow is Wednesday. I'll wake up and the coffee will be bad and my mother will call and I'll drive past the house and I'll stand in the kitchen eating from the pot and I'll read pages of someone else's story before sleep pulls me under.

And maybe that's enough. Maybe that's what Tuesdays are for—not for meaning, but for getting through. For pressing on the bruise and confirming: still here. Still dropping through air. Still meaning it.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to write freely - 1000 words, whatever comes to me. Let me just write from an authentic place. No pretense, no trying to be impressive. Just write.

Let me see what emerges.
